metadata.title: One Little Sun: How We Built an AI Numbers Song for Early Learners

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Abstract numbers mean little to young children until they attach to concrete objects worth returning to. The song ties each number to something tangible so it becomes memorable rather than arbitrary. The audio aesthetic was chosen to support that learning, not merely to sound pleasant. The body of the song is itself part of the lesson, carrying the counting mechanism inside it.

artifactLines: Abstract numbers mean little to young children until they attach to concrete objects worth returning to.
The song ties each number to something tangible so it becomes memorable rather than arbitrary.
The audio aesthetic was chosen to support that learning, not merely to sound pleasant.
The body of the song is itself part of the lesson, carrying the counting mechanism inside it.

beats.narration_text: I keep circling back to how "four" only becomes memorable once it's tied to something concrete a toddler can return to, and how the learning mechanism was folded into both the body and the audio. I want to try building one number's worth of that for early learners. For the number four specifically, can you (1) name three concrete recurring objects a two-year-old already knows that could anchor "four"; (2) sketch how the melody or rhythm could reinforce the count without leaning on words; (3) explain what makes a child want to return to it?
